perturbation.original_text: (e) " Restricted Period " means the period commencing on the date of this Agreement and ending on the date which is five (5) years from the date hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: (e) " Restricted Period " means the period commencing on the date of this Agreement and ending on the date which is ten (10) years from the date hereof.
perturbation.explanation: Increasing the non-compete period to 10 years may be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable under California law, as California generally disfavors non-compete agreements. While this agreement is governed by Massachusetts law, the seller (Accurel) is a California corporation, raising the potential for a conflict of laws analysis where California law might be applied, especially if the seller's employees are based in California.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. While there are exceptions, a 10-year non-compete is likely to be viewed as an unreasonable restraint, especially given the broad scope of the agreement and the potential applicability of California law due to Accurel's location.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the non-compete period from five to ten years. This directly contradicts California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, as stated in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void," with broad interpretation according to Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937. The longer period strengthens the restraint, thus creating a clear contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 10. Notices. Any notice or other communication required or permitted to be delivered to any party shall be in writing and shall be deemed properly delivered, given and received when delivered, by hand, by registered mail, by courier or express delivery service, by facsimile, or by e-mail to the address or facsimile number set forth beneath the name of such party below, or to such other address or facsimile number as such party shall have specified in a written notice given to the other parties:
perturbation.changed_text: 10. Notices. Any notice or other communication required or permitted to be delivered to any party shall be in writing and shall be deemed properly delivered, given and received only when delivered, by facsimile to the facsimile number set forth beneath the name of such party below, or to such other facsimile number as such party shall have specified in a written notice given to the other parties:
perturbation.explanation: Restricting acceptable notice methods solely to facsimile may contradict the Uniform Electronic Transactions Act (UETA), adopted by many states, including Massachusetts, which gives legal recognition to electronic records and signatures. Excluding other methods such as email could render notices invalid under UETA if they meet UETA's requirements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 110G (Uniform Electronic Transactions Act)
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 110G, adopting the Uniform Electronic Transactions Act, generally validates electronic records and signatures. By restricting notice solely to facsimile, the modified text conflicts with UETA's principle of technological neutrality and could invalidate notices sent electronically that would otherwise be valid under UETA.
perturbation.justification: The changed text restricts acceptable notice methods solely to facsimile. The Perturbation and Law Explanations state that this contradicts the Uniform Electronic Transactions Act (UETA), which gives legal recognition to electronic records and signatures. By limiting notices to facsimile, the modified text conflicts with UETA's principle of technological neutrality and could invalidate notices sent electronically that would otherwise be valid under UETA. The absence of scraped law snippets does not change the fact that the change clearly violates UETA principles as stated by the provided explanations.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be construed in accordance with, and governed in all respects by, the internal laws of the State of Massachusetts, without giving effect to principles of conflicts of laws.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be construed in accordance with, and governed in all respects by, the internal laws of the State of Delaware, without giving effect to principles of conflicts of laws.
perturbation.explanation: While parties are generally free to choose the governing law, selecting Delaware law when the primary parties (Seller - CA, Guarantor - MA, Buyer - DE) have strong connections to other states, and the contract is about the sale of assets located elsewhere, could be challenged if it violates a fundamental policy of a state with a materially greater interest in the dispute. For instance, if the non-compete provisions are overly broad under California law, a California court might refuse to enforce them despite the Delaware choice-of-law clause.
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187 outlines limitations on choice-of-law provisions. Specifically, it states that the chosen law will not be applied if it violates a fundamental policy of a state which has a materially greater interest than the chosen state in the determination of the particular issue and which would be the state of the applicable law in the absence of an effective choice of law by the parties.

perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces Massachusetts law with Delaware law as the governing law. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation both highlight that this choice of law can be problematic if it violates a fundamental policy of a state with a materially greater interest. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is 'None', Scraped Law Snippet 2 includes news about legal tech and does not pertain to the legal question about choice of law. Even so, the Perturbation explanation explicitly says that selecting Delaware law, given the circumstances, 'could be challenged if it violates a fundamental policy of a state with a materially greater interest in the dispute.' This creates a potential legal contradiction, making the contract unenforceable under certain conditions, thus the categorization is YES.

perturbation.original_text: 9. Governing Law; Venue. (a) This Agreement shall be construed in accordance with, and governed in all respects by, the internal laws of the State of Massachusetts, without giving effect to principles of conflicts of laws. (b) Unless otherwise explicitly provided in this Agreement, any Proceeding relating to this Agreement or the enforcement of any provision of this Agreement may be brought or otherwise commenced in any state or federal court located in the County of Middlesex, Massachusetts.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. Governing Law; Venue. (a) This Agreement shall be construed in accordance with, and governed in all respects by, the internal laws of the State of Massachusetts, without giving effect to principles of conflicts of laws. (b) Unless otherwise explicitly provided in this Agreement, any Proceeding relating to this Agreement or the enforcement of any provision of this Agreement must be brought or otherwise commenced exclusively in the courts of North Korea.
perturbation.explanation: Designating North Korea as the exclusive forum for dispute resolution is likely unenforceable under U.S. law and public policy due to practical impossibility, lack of due process, and potential violation of sanctions. Forum selection clauses are generally enforced, but not if they are unreasonable or unjust.
perturbation.law_explanation: 22 U.S.C. § 9001 and related sanctions laws impose restrictions on transactions and dealings with North Korea. While this statute doesn't directly address forum selection, designating North Korea as the exclusive forum creates practical and legal obstacles for U.S. entities to pursue legal action, effectively undermining their rights and potentially violating U.S. foreign policy interests.

(2) Persons should not be permitted to obtain custody of children by virtue of their wrongful removal or retention. (3) International abductions and retentions of children are increasing, and only concerted cooperation pursuant to an internation
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text designates North Korea as the exclusive forum for dispute resolution, which contradicts U.S. law and policy. While 22 U.S.C. § 9001, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, primarily addresses international child abduction, the Law Explanation broadens the scope to include restrictions on dealings with North Korea. Designating North Korea as the exclusive forum creates practical and legal obstacles, potentially violating U.S. foreign policy interests. The Perturbation Explanation also notes the practical impossibility, lack of due process, and potential violation of sanctions, all of which underscore the unenforceability of the modified clause under U.S. law. This falls under the Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition, as the venue requirement violates federal law, making the contractual term unenforceable.
